name: Tohil
description: Tohil was a major deity of the K'iche' Maya in the Late Postclassic period. He was primarily a god of fire, rain, and thunder, and was closely associated with warfare and sacrifice. Tohil was considered a patron god of the K'iche' people, and his image was housed in their capital city, Q'umarkaj. He was often depicted as a flint knife or wrapped in feathers, symbolizing his connection to both sacrifice and power. According to the Popol Vuh, the K'iche' Maya received fire from Tohil, making him a vital figure in their creation myth. He demanded blood sacrifice and was seen as a powerful protector in exchange for offerings. Tohil's importance diminished after the Spanish conquest, but he remains a significant figure in the study of Mayan religion and culture, representing the complex relationship between deities, warfare, and the natural world in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica. He was also considered a solar deity, further linking him to vital life forces.
subtitle: God of Fire, Rain, and Sacrifice

keyMyths.title: The Gift of Fire
keyMyths.description: Tohil provided fire to the K'iche' people, a crucial event described in the Popol Vuh. This act solidified his position as a primary deity and benefactor.

keyMyths.title: Demands for Sacrifice
keyMyths.description: Tohil required blood sacrifice from the K'iche' in exchange for his protection and blessings. This highlights the reciprocal relationship between the deity and his followers.

keyMyths.title: Patron of Warfare
keyMyths.description: Tohil's association with warfare made him a key figure in K'iche' military endeavors. Victories were often attributed to his favor and support.
keyMyths.source: Various Mayan codices and historical accounts
